title: Unable to run configure script in a single threaded environment
body:
I'm working in an environment where `libc++` and `libc++abi` have been built without support for threads. 

When I try and checkout `v4.216` of Verilator and build this from source I hit a configure error which states that `"Verilator requires a C++11 or newer compiler.`". When I trace this back through the `configure.ac` script I see that on [line 485](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/blob/434c3c3ef3ae751b0a9f2d5934c2d2e986e65e81/configure.ac#L485) it is generating a test program that looks something like this:

```c
#include <thread>
#if (__cplusplus < 201103L)
# error "Too old"
#endif

int main()
{
    return 0;
}
```

The particular issue I am facing is that `<thread>` does not exist on my system because `libc++` was not compiled with threading support enabled. 

However, I have patched `configure.ac` locally to remove the `#include <thread>` and things seem to then work fine. After looking through the history of this part of `configure.ac` it seems that this [commit](https://github.com/verilator/verilator/commit/698e0fbbd1e866dc8abaa4900a1b56ca7316378b) led to this change. My understanding of this is that prior to that change it was only looking for C++11 support if threading was enabled, in which case it made sense to check if the `<thread>` header existed on the system. It now seems that C++11 support is required unconditionally and so I'm wondering if this check should now omit the call to `#include <thread>` and instead just check the `__cplusplus` macro value only?

I'm happy to submit my patch as a PR for this if it's a genuine issue. However, if I've misunderstood something then I'd be curious to know if building Verilator on a single threaded environment is something that is suppported.

What OS and distribution are you using?

Alpine 3.14.2 via a Docker image
